summary: Do not ship this plan yet: core recovery behavior lacks executable acceptance paths, the Renovate story cannot achieve its stated outcome, and the risk-bearing slice order is undefined. No materially simpler approach was found that preserves all confirmed requirements.

findings.title: Open question: which seam tests database failover recovery?
findings.body: The only specified API readiness test exercises the no-database branch and explicitly excludes database failure. It therefore never triggers the new pool error listener or proves the promised sequence: process remains alive, liveness stays 200, readiness becomes 503, and readiness recovers after reconnection. The central failure could remain unchanged while every planned test passes.
findings.recommendation: Name an executable seam that induces an idle-connection failure. Prefer an image-level test with temporary Postgres/PgBouncer that asserts liveness 200, readiness 503, process survival, and eventual readiness 200; otherwise define an injected-pool test covering the same transitions.

findings.title: Open question: which seam tests worker lifecycle behavior?
findings.body: The per-archetype smoke contract checks only the worker startup log. That passes with the current broken sleep behavior and cannot exercise transient-error backoff or fatal-error propagation. The generic shutdown paragraph does not resolve this explicit worker definition, and the plan never defines how transient and fatal errors are distinguished.
findings.recommendation: Require the worker image test to send SIGTERM during sleep and assert bounded exit time, exit code 0, and the stopped log. Define the fatal/transient classification and add an injectable task or process-level fixture that verifies transient backoff and fatal propagation.

findings.title: Renovate configuration cannot deliver the promised security updates
findings.body: The plan bundles configuration while excluding repository enrollment. The existing homelab Renovate workflow enumerates only homelab and homelab-app-template with autodiscovery disabled, so a generated repository's configuration is never read. Consequently the user story's security-update outcome remains false at completion; an untracked future follow-up is not an operational dependency.
findings.recommendation: Either include repository enrollment as a required cross-repository dependency before declaring the lifecycle story done, or narrow the story to Renovate readiness and record a concrete follow-up with owner, tracker, ordering, and acceptance criteria.

findings.title: Walking-skeleton and dependency order are undefined
findings.body: The plan lists roughly seven slices but identifies neither the structure-gate skeleton nor dependency edges. The test-structure/Dockerfile-gate slice establishes the hardest-to-reverse forked contract, while template-ci shutdown verification depends on runtime shutdown work. Unordered issue generation can front-load easy documentation, create overlapping server edits, or attempt acceptance tests before their behavior exists.
findings.recommendation: Designate the risk-bearing skeleton explicitly and state the issue DAG. The skeleton should exercise one generated archetype through app export, Dockerfile gates, image startup, and shutdown; make remaining runtime and template-ci slices depend on the relevant seam or behavior slices.

findings.title: Open question: which seam tests fullstack development orchestration?
findings.body: The plan promises that one command starts both processes, preserves the API proxy, prefixes logs, and terminates both children, but no Testing Decision exercises that process behavior. Merely asserting that a generated script exists would allow the original DX failure to be replaced by a command that starts incompletely, loses proxying, or leaks a child process.
findings.recommendation: Add a generated-app process seam in template-ci: run `bun run dev`, wait for the web and API endpoints, verify a proxied API request, terminate the parent, and assert both child processes exit.

next_steps: Revise the Testing Decisions and acceptance criteria for DB recovery, worker lifecycle behavior, and fullstack development.
Reconcile the Renovate user story with its explicitly deferred enrollment dependency.
Mark the walking skeleton and issue dependency order, then rerun the plan review.
